title: What is a Database Management System?
body: A DBMS is software that manages databases and provides mechanisms to store and retrieve data efficiently. It handles data organization and ensures data integrity.

title: Why do we need a DBMS?
body: List three problems without DBMS: data redundancy, data inconsistency, difficulty in data access and security issues.

title: DBMS vs File System
body: File systems store unstructured data; DBMS provides structured data management with queries and concurrent access.

title: Advantages of DBMS
body: Data independence, data security, concurrent access, recovery mechanisms, and query optimization.

title: Data Models in DBMS
body: Hierarchical (tree structure) vs Network (graph) vs Relational (tables) vs NoSQL (document/key-value).

title: Relational Model fundamentals
body: Relations as tables; attributes as columns; tuples as rows; keys for uniqueness and relationships.

title: Entity Relationship Model
body: ER diagrams represent entities (objects) and relationships between them; basis for database design.

title: Introduction to SQL
body: SQL is declarative language for CRUD operations: CREATE READ UPDATE DELETE on relational databases.

title: SQL SELECT statement
body: SELECT retrieves data from tables with WHERE conditions, ORDER BY, GROUP BY, and aggregate functions.

title: Joins in SQL
body: INNER JOIN (matching rows), LEFT JOIN (all left + matching), RIGHT JOIN, FULL OUTER JOIN connect multiple tables.

title: Normalization basics
body: Normalization eliminates redundancy through 1NF, 2NF, 3NF; reduces data anomalies and dependency issues.

title: First Normal Form (1NF)
body: All attributes contain atomic values; no repeating groups or arrays in single fields.

title: Second Normal Form (2NF)
body: Candidate key determines all non-prime attributes; remove partial dependencies between attributes and key.

title: Third Normal Form (3NF)
body: Non-prime attributes depend only on candidate key; remove transitive dependencies among non-prime attributes.

title: ACID properties
body: Atomicity (all-or-nothing), Consistency (valid state), Isolation (concurrent transactions), Durability (persistent).

title: Transactions and ACID
body: Explain how banks ensure ACID properties during fund transfers between accounts.

title: Concurrency Control basics
body: Multiple users accessing database simultaneously; prevent conflicts like lost updates and dirty reads.

title: Lock-based protocols
body: Shared locks for reads; exclusive locks for writes. Deadlock occurs when processes wait circularly.

title: Indexing and Performance
body: Indexes speed up data retrieval like book index; types include primary index, clustered index, and secondary index.

title: Hash Indexing
body: Hash function maps key to bucket location; O(1) search but poor for range queries.

title: B-Tree and B+ Tree Index
body: Balanced tree structures maintain sorted order. B+ Tree uses leaf pointers for range queries; used in most databases.

title: Query Optimization
body: Optimizer estimates cost of query plans and chooses efficient execution path; uses indexes and statistics.

title: Backup and Recovery
body: Backup copies of database; Recovery restores after failure using logs and checkpoints; WAL (Write-Ahead Logging).

title: Data Replication
body: Copies database to multiple servers for availability and fault tolerance; master-slave or peer-to-peer replication.

title: DBMS Architecture layers
body: External (views), Conceptual (logical schema), Internal (physical storage) - three-level architecture.

title: Storage Management
body: Buffer management, disk I/O optimization, record organization (heap, sequential, indexed-sequential) and access.

title: NoSQL Databases
body: Non-relational databases: document (MongoDB), key-value (Redis), graph (Neo4j), column-family (Cassandra).

title: MongoDB basics
body: Document-oriented database; flexible schema; JSON-like documents; horizontal scalability with sharding.

title: Distributed Databases
body: Data spread across multiple locations; challenges: consistency, eventual consistency, CAP theorem.

title: CAP Theorem
body: Cannot guarantee all three: Consistency, Availability, Partition tolerance. Trade-offs in distributed systems.
